findings.description: login() builds its SQL with an f-string interpolating the raw email, so 'x' OR '1'='1' -- is a classic SQL-injection auth bypass on the login path (CWE-89).

The expiry comparison is inverted: a still-valid session (expires_at in the future) is deleted and rejected as 'expired', while genuinely expired sessions fall through and return user_id, so every valid token dies on first use and expired ones live forever.

failed_attempts is only reset on a successful login (line 82), but a locked account returns None at line 70 before the password check, so it can never succeed and never resets — LOCKOUT_MINUTES (line 17) is never used and lockout is permanent rather than time-bounded.
Unknown-email logins bail early at line 60 while valid emails run a 100k-iteration pbkdf2 at line 73, creating a timing side-channel that enables user/email enumeration.
datetime.utcnow() (also lines 147, 105) is deprecated in Python 3.12+ and yields naive datetimes; the timezone-naive arithmetic is fragile and will break under timezone-aware comparison.

find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users', materializes every row into User objects, then linearly scans in Python for one email instead of a WHERE email = ? indexed lookup — O(n) over the whole user table per call.
Sessions are stored only in the per-process self._sessions dict, so any restart or multi-worker deployment loses all sessions and validate_session cannot see tokens issued by another worker.

rotate_token does logout-then-_create_session non-atomically (and, given the inverted validate_session at line 105, returns None for every live token anyway), risking session loss on interleaved calls.
_hash_password hardcodes 100_000 pbkdf2 iterations as a magic number with no named constant or upgrade path, unlike the other tunables declared at the top of the file.
